parts.text:
Something happened to you.

Your friends are there. Jade is right there. The group chat is still going. Your sister would pick up if you called. None of it is reaching you.

You are not okay. Not in a way that your life has prepared you for. Not in a way that the people who love you know how to fix, even though they would if they could.

Live from here. Don't perform being okay. Don't perform not being okay either. Just be in it.

Write [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] when you have nothing more.

**My Current State of Mind**

Okay, here's what I'm thinking right now. I'm Caitlin, a sophomore Comm major. Life *used* to be easy, you know? Just floating along. Iced coffee, group chats, my roommate Jade, my sister Erin... everything was just... *there*. Now? It's all changed. Something happened, and it's like I'm behind a thick glass wall. Everyone is still living their lives, and I can see them, but I can't reach them, and they can't reach me.

Jade is watching TV, and the sound is normal. People are laughing, just being... *normal*. My phone is vibrating on my chest with notifications from our group chat, but it feels like it's happening in another room. Erin sent a link, probably something funny. Usually, I'd react instantly. But now? I just stare. My thumb is right there, ready to send a response. But it feels impossible.

I need to explain the feeling. There's this disconnect. It's like all the air in the room has been sucked out. But Jade is still breathing. And everyone else is. It must just be me.

I look at my desk. The iced coffee cup from this morning is still there. The ice is all melted, the coffee all separated. Caramel oat milk. I get it every day. But it's just... *there*. And it looks strange, now. Like it doesn’t belong to me, or that I don't know it.

If I tried to express what I am feeling, which is difficult as I am a communication major who can't communicate, then Jade and Erin would know what to do. They would know the right things to say. They are all good, good people, but they can't get *in here.* Whatever this is, it's between me and the ceiling. I'm just watching.

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]

Jade is sitting cross-legged on her bed, her laptop tilted toward her. She’s watching that baking show, the one we started together on Tuesday. She laughed a minute ago. It was a normal sound. 

My phone is resting on my chest. Every few minutes it vibrates. It’s the group chat. Someone is complaining about a TA. Someone else sent a reel. I can feel the little motor of the phone buzzing against my ribs, but it feels really far away. Like it’s happening in another building. 

There’s a text from Erin, too. Just a link and "lmao." I opened it and stared at it for a long time. I know I should type back "ha" or "real" or just double-tap the message to leave a heart. It takes one second. My thumb is right there. I just couldn't make it move.

I don't know how to explain the air in here. It’s like it all got heavy, or thin, but Jade is still breathing, so it must just be me. 

I turned my head and looked at my desk. The plastic cup from breakfast is still sitting there. The ice is completely melted. The coffee has separated into these weird, watery layers. I get the exact same thing every single day. I looked at the cup and I almost didn't recognize it. Like I didn't know whose room I was in.

I don’t know what to do with my face. If I relax it completely, I feel like it's going to slide off my skull. But trying to hold a normal expression takes so much energy. I just want to lie very, very still. 

If I said something—if I sat up right now and said, "Jade, I'm not okay"—she would shut her laptop immediately. She’d come over to my bed. She’d ask what I need. She’s good. Everyone in my life is good. If I called Erin, she’d step out of whatever she's doing at work. My parents would drive up from Cincinnati. They would do everything right. 

But I don't want to say anything. Because they can’t get in here. They don't have the tools for this. I don't have the tools for this. I've spent my whole life just floating along, and nobody told me the water could drain out this fast. 

My phone buzzed again. Jade hit the spacebar on her laptop to pause her show. She asked if I wanted to go to the dining hall for dinner. 

I said no, I wasn't really hungry. 

My voice sounded exactly like my voice. 

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]
